title: BFS and DFS - two ways to explore
author: Prodipta Acharjee
concept: Breadth-first search (BFS) explores a graph level by level using a queue; depth-first search (DFS) goes as deep as it can before backtracking, using a stack or recursion.
explanation: Once you have a graph, you need to walk it without going in circles (so you track what you've seen). The order is the choice. BFS spreads out evenly - all the immediate neighbours, then their neighbours - which is how it finds the shortest path in an unweighted graph; it uses a queue. DFS plunges down one path to the end, then backs up and tries another; it uses a stack, or just recursion. Same nodes, very different journeys.

exercises.question: What does this BFS print?
hints: BFS visits a, then a's neighbours b and c, then b's neighbour d.
Level by level, left to right.

exercises.question: Breadth-first search explores using a...
hints: First-in, first-out keeps it level by level.
The code used a deque as one.

exercises.question: Depth-first search goes ___ before backtracking.
options: as deep as possible down a path
hints: The 'depth' in the name is the clue.
It plunges down a single path first.
As deep as possible, then backs up.
